chunk_summary: This section outlines criteria for triaging children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) into inpatient or outpatient care based on the presence of complications, danger signs, and caregiver capacity.
learning_objectives: Differentiate between complicated and uncomplicated SAM based on clinical criteria.
Understand the role of danger signs and appetite tests in SAM triage decisions.
Recognize the importance of caregiver capacity and outpatient program reliability in treatment planning.
key_facts: Complicated SAM includes medical complications such as hypoglycemia, hypothermia, fever ≥39°C, or severe oedema (+++).
Uncomplicated SAM requires clinical stability, no danger signs, and a passed appetite test.
Severe oedema (+++) is an automatic inpatient indication in Indian protocols unless outpatient programs explicitly monitor oedema.
Caregiver barriers, such as inability to administer feeds or unsafe hygiene, justify inpatient care.
WHO 2023 guidelines allow trained community teams to treat selected children with nutritional oedema outside hospitals.
reference_quote: Severe oedema (+++) is an automatic inpatient indication in Indian protocols. The 2023 WHO guideline allows trained community teams to treat selected children with nutritional oedema outside hospital, but outpatient system reliability varies across India.
document_key_topics: Role of oedema and caregiver capacity in SAM management
